title: Where Does Quantum Computing Stand?
gist: A survey of the quantum-computing industry's state as of mid-2026: it is emerging slowly from research toward production, with 132 QED-C member companies, no settled qubit modality, and commercial timelines ranging from 3-5 years to the 2040s-2050s. Near-term value is concentrated in cryptography (post-quantum defense against Shor's algorithm), while quantum is positioned as an accelerator (QPU) alongside CPUs and GPUs rather than a general-purpose replacement.

insights.claim: The Quantum Economic Development Consortium (QED-C) has 132 member companies versus only 36 universities/independent research centers and 11 federally funded research centers working on quantum, indicating commercial entrants now outnumber academic ones in an industry still finding its footing.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Commercial density outpacing academic base suggests capital is flowing ahead of settled science - a fragmentation/shakeout risk to underwrite.
insights.quote: So far, QED-C has 132 member companies, which is a lot for an industry still finding its footing.
insights.locator: The state of the quantum computing industry

insights.claim: The quantum industry is currently vertically integrated with production confined to R&D-scale, wholly-owned facilities, comparable to where semiconductors were in the 1970s-80s, with no foundries even a tenth the scale of TSMC.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Absence of a fabless/foundry split means a decade-plus of infrastructure buildout before scale economics arrive - long-duration capital.
insights.quote: The quantum industry is in a state of vertical integration, like semiconductors were in the '80s or '70s.
insights.locator: Finally, when all research and development is complete

insights.claim: The quantum computing field has not converged on a single qubit modality, with superconducting, photonic, atom-based, and ion-based 'transistor' types all still competing and no clear winner, and the author suggests more than one modality may ultimately survive.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Modality uncertainty is a core diversification risk - bets on any single qubit technology carry high obsolescence risk.
insights.quote: it hasn't become clear which of these modalities is going to get the biggest slice of the pie. And there may be more than one slice.

insights.claim: Quantum computing is best framed as an accelerator (a QPU) directed by conventional high-performance computers, following the historical CPU-then-GPU-then-QPU pattern, rather than a paradigm that overruns classical computing.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Positions quantum as an adjunct to HPC/data-center stacks, not a disruptor of classical compute demand - relevant to how hyperscalers and HPC vendors integrate it.
insights.quote: We had CPUs, then we added GPUs, and now we're going to add QPUs.
insights.locator: Some see quantum computers as accelerators

insights.claim: Application-specific quantum platforms built for narrow, high-value problems (military or pharma) will likely arrive before any fault-tolerant general-purpose quantum computer.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Early revenue will concentrate in vertical, whole-stack systems for defense and drug discovery - where to look for first commercial traction.
insights.quote: there will likely be more application-specific platforms with the whole stack designed to do something that the military cares about or drug companies have decided they're willing to pay for
insights.locator: Even if a general-purpose quantum computer

insights.claim: Quantum computers will almost certainly remain confined to data centers rather than handheld devices unless room-temperature operation is achieved, so most users will subscribe to quantum-computing services rather than own hardware.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A cloud/as-a-service business model is the default distribution path - favors hyperscalers and specialized quantum-cloud providers over on-prem hardware sales.
insights.quote: We're unlikely to go through a phase where every enterprise has its own quantum computer in a closet somewhere

themes.theme: Cryptography as the one certain use case

insights.claim: Shor's algorithm's ability to factor large numbers is the one certain quantum use case and will undermine current public-key cryptography, making post-quantum cryptography and a 'quantum-safe baseline' the primary near-term quantum effort.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Post-quantum crypto migration is the clearest actionable near-term market, driven by 'harvest-now-decrypt-later' threat urgency.
insights.quote: The one certain thing is Shor's algorithm, which factors large numbers, will undermine the current public-key approach to security.

insights.claim: The 'harvest now, decrypt later' threat is already live, with adversaries downloading and storing encrypted documents today in expectation of decrypting them once quantum matures, driving urgency for quantum-safe cryptography now.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Creates present-day demand for PQC regardless of when quantum hardware arrives - a defensive-security spend tailwind.
insights.quote: people download and store encrypted documents in the expectation of being able to decrypt them later

insights.claim: Breaking 256-bit keys via Shor's algorithm may require millions of qubits to remain robust against noise, and expectations for that milestone partly explain forecasts pushing widespread quantum to the 2040s-2050s.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: The qubit-count gap (millions vs. ~128 today) frames the multi-decade runway for cryptographically-relevant quantum.
insights.quote: Some say Shor's algorithm will require millions of qubits to remain robust against noise.

themes.theme: Hardware benchmarks and the cooling problem

insights.claim: IBM recently announced entangling 128 superconducting qubits with fidelity above 0.5, cited as a good snapshot of the current state of the art but still short of what commercial deployment requires.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A concrete progress benchmark (128 qubits >0.5 fidelity) to gauge the gap to commercial viability.
insights.quote: IBM recently announced entangling 128 superconducting qubits with fidelity above 0.5. This is a good snapshot of where the world stands today.

insights.claim: Neutral-atom experiments have loaded over 10,000 atoms that can be controlled to some extent, presenting a promising scaling path relative to the ~128-qubit superconducting benchmark.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Neutral-atom scale (10,000+ atoms) is a competitive-threat data point versus superconducting incumbents like IBM.
insights.quote: experiments with neutral atoms [another qubit option] loaded over 10,000 atoms that can be controlled to some extent

insights.claim: Quantum's low-power promise is undercut by cooling costs, since superconducting qubits operate at 0.04 K and require dilution refrigerators, meaning energy saved in computation is spent on cryogenics.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Cryogenic overhead is a structural cost/efficiency barrier that gates data-center economics of superconducting quantum.
insights.quote: Superconducting qubits operate at 0.04 K, and this is unlikely to change in the foreseeable future

insights.claim: IBM's superconducting qubits are unstable because they are extremely sensitive to temperature and electrical disturbances, a fundamental reliability limitation of that modality.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Instability is the core technical risk for the leading superconducting approach - a wedge for competing modalities.
insights.quote: because they're so sensitive to temperature and electrical disturbances, they're not stable

insights.claim: Intel and others have bet heavily on spin-based qubits specifically because they can be manufactured using standard semiconductor fab capabilities, tying quantum scaling to existing CMOS manufacturing prowess.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Fab-compatible spin qubits give incumbent chipmakers (Intel) a potential manufacturing edge if the modality proves viable.
insights.quote: companies like Intel have pinned a lot of hopes on being able to manufacture a lot of them
insights.locator: Multiple qubit technologies remain in play

insights.claim: Startup Quobly has a chip with co-integrated control electronics and quantum logic operating between 500 mK and 1 K, and adapting its process design kit required only low-temperature characterization, letting designers reuse much existing IP by tweaking dimensions.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: IP reuse via low-temperature-characterized PDKs lowers the design barrier for CMOS-quantum integration - a route that leverages existing EDA/IP ecosystems.
insights.quote: We did characterization at low temperature, and that's the only thing that changed in the PDK

themes.theme: The three unsolved legs: qubits, error correction, software

insights.claim: Quantum viability requires advances across three areas simultaneously - making good qubits and connecting them, error correction, and algorithms/software - so progress in any single dimension is insufficient.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A three-legged framework for tracking readiness; bottleneck in any leg gates the whole system.
insights.quote: There are three areas where advancement is going to have to be made
insights.locator: Achieving a viable, scalable hardware platform

insights.claim: Surface codes predominate for quantum error correction today but scale poorly, and the industry has not settled on which logical-qubit codes to standardize on - a major unresolved gap.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Lack of a converged error-correction standard is a structural blocker to scale and interoperability.
insights.quote: So-called surface codes predominate now but don't scale well.

insights.claim: There is no quantitative performance benchmark for quantum analogous to gigaflops/teraflops in HPC, making progress measurement inherently fuzzy and complicating investor and buyer evaluation.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Absence of a standard metric makes vendor claims hard to compare - a due-diligence hazard.
insights.quote: There's no analog to the high-performance computing benchmarks such as gigaflops or teraflops or petaflops

themes.theme: Timeline and the AI-style breakthrough wildcard

insights.claim: A QED-C membership survey found about half expect a commercial quantum offering in three to five years and about a third expect more than five years, revealing wide divergence in industry timing expectations.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Split-timeline consensus (3-5 yrs vs 5+ yrs) quantifies the uncertainty band for commercial quantum bets.
insights.quote: About half said three to five years, and about a third said more than five years

insights.claim: Sentiment is accelerating: a quantum-physicist founder who two-three years ago estimated 10-15+ years to a useful quantum computer now estimates three to five years, suggesting perceived timelines are compressing.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Compressing insider timelines are a leading sentiment indicator that could pull forward capital and expectations.
insights.quote: Now here we are, just a couple years later, and if you ask me this question, I will say three to five years.
insights.locator: Some folks see things speeding up

insights.claim: The author frames an LLM-style breakthrough as the key wildcard that could suddenly accelerate quantum much as LLMs jolted AI, while noting such discontinuities are by definition unpredictable.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Frames the asymmetric upside case - a single breakthrough could reprice timelines, arguing for optionality rather than conviction.
insights.quote: It may be that some big discovery changes the pace of things, much as LLMs changed the pace of AI development
insights.locator: For now, research and development proceed

insights.claim: A SEMI advisor ranks quantum as 'second in line behind photonics' in terms of making a big impact, implying photonics/co-packaged optics will deliver commercial value materially sooner than quantum.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Prioritizes photonics over quantum for near-term returns - a relative-timing call favoring silicon photonics plays.
insights.quote: Quantum, in my naive view, is standing second in line behind photonics in terms of making a big impact
insights.locator: But it will be worth waiting for
